title: Spectral Minimal Partitions: Local vs Global Minimality
speaker: Gregory Berkolaiko
abstract:
In this overview talk we will explore a variational approach to the problem of Spectral Minimal Partitions (SMPs).  The problem is to partition a domain or a manifold into k subdomains so that the first Dirichlet eigenvalue on each subdomain is as small as possible.  We expand the problem to consider Spectral Critical Partitions (partitions where the max of the Dirichlet eigenvalues is experiencing a critical point) and show that a locally minimal bipartite partition is automatically globally minimal. Extensions of this result to non-bipartite partitions, as well as its connections to counting nodal domains of the eigenfunctions and to a two-sided Dirichlet-to-Neumann map defined on the partition boundaries, will also be discussed.
The talk is based on joint work with Yaiza Canzani, Graham Cox, Bernard Helffer, Peter Kuchment, Jeremy Marzuola, Uzy Smilansky and Mikael Sundqvist, with and without the speaker.

proof_stages.label: Spectral partition variational problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines a compact planar domain or surface, Dirichlet eigenvalues, k-partitions into open subdomains, the max-first-eigenvalue energy, and global spectral minimal partitions, together with smoothness and bipartiteness terminology.

proof_stages.label: Equipartition and nodal-partition candidates
proof_stages.summary: Hadamard boundary variation suggests that minimizers should be equipartitions, while nodal domains of real Laplace eigenfunctions give bipartite equipartitions governed by Courant's bound and the nodal deficiency n−ν_n.

proof_stages.label: Local deformation space for equipartitions
proof_stages.summary: To make local minimum, critical point, and Hessian precise, the speaker uses a Hilbert-manifold neighborhood of H^s diffeomorphisms of the domain that preserve the equipartition constraint.

proof_stages.label: Hessian as a Dirichlet-to-Neumann form
proof_stages.summary: The second variation of the partition energy is identified with a weighted Dirichlet-to-Neumann quadratic form, and the vanishing of the weight at multiple junctions explains the non-closed form and the appearance of singular topology-breaking directions.

proof_stages.label: Critical points and Morse index equal nodal deficiency
proof_stages.summary: For bipartite equipartitions, nodal partitions of the nth eigenfunction are exactly the critical points, and their Morse index equals the nodal deficiency n−ν_n; conversely the index recovers the eigenfunction number.

proof_stages.label: Bipartite local-global minimality theorem
proof_stages.summary: Combining the critical-point theorem with known results on Courant-sharp nodal partitions yields the equivalence: bipartite local minimum, global k-spectral minimal partition, and nodal partition of the kth Courant-sharp eigenfunction.

proof_stages.label: Non-bipartite modification and topological classes
proof_stages.summary: For non-bipartite partitions the ordinary Laplacian is replaced by equivalent Aharonov-Bohm or branched-cover Laplacians, with Z2 homology encoding non-bipartiteness and supporting a conjectural local-equals-global principle within topological classes.

examples.name: 1 by sqrt(2) rectangle with a quadruple-cross partition
examples.note: The rectangle partition generated by the fifth eigenfunction has nodal deficiency one but cannot be deformed by a diffeomorphism into the numerically expected minimizer, revealing why singular topology-breaking directions must be encoded through the Hessian/Dirichlet-to-Neumann analysis.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies the theory to the 1 by sqrt(2) rectangle, identifies the eigenfunction index, computes the nodal deficiency as one, and analyzes the resulting deformation issue at the quadruple point

passes.reason: The regular quadruple-cross nodal partition is a collapsed symmetric saddle/critical case whose fourfold point obstructs deformation by diffeomorphisms.
